skill.name: hig-components-dialogs
skill.description: Apple Human Interface Guidelines guidance for alerts, action sheets, popovers, sheets, and digit entry views.
skill.summary: Choose and design Apple-style presentation components for modal and nonmodal flows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but manual review found they are false positives from Markdown documentation. The apparent command execution is inline API names in backticks, and the hardcoded URLs point to Apple documentation or image assets. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential handling, or executable code was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown API References Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged backticks as Ruby or shell execution. These locations are Markdown inline code references to Apple APIs or a local context filename, not executable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are Markdown reference documents. The flagged backtick content names Apple framework APIs and does not invoke a shell, interpreter, or external process.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation URLs Flagged as Network Risk
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are source links, cross-reference links, and image links to Apple Developer documentation. The skill contains no runtime network request logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear inside Markdown front matter, links, and image references. No fetch, curl, socket, webhook, telemetry, or exfiltration behavior is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Malware Keywords From Documentation Text
low_findings.description: Weak crypto, C2, and reconnaissance matches came from Apple asset hashes, ordinary design language, and documentation examples. No cryptographic code, C2 behavior, or reconnaissance logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review ties the suspicious tokens to Markdown prose, image URLs, and metadata. There is no executable implementation using crypto, networking control channels, or system discovery.

content.value_statement: Teams often choose intrusive dialogs when a lighter presentation would work better. This skill maps the task to Apple HIG guidance for alerts, sheets, popovers, action sheets, and digit entry views.
actual_capabilities: Recommends alerts, sheets, popovers, action sheets, or digit entry views based on user intent.
Explains when a modal interruption is appropriate and when inline feedback is better.
Provides Apple-style guidance for titles, messages, button labels, and dismiss behavior.
Covers platform differences for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS.
Flags destructive actions and recommends safe Cancel or undo patterns.
References related HIG areas for menus, controls, search, modality, and foundations.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not generate production UI components by itself.
It reflects the included reference files and may not include newer Apple updates.
It focuses on presentation components, not full navigation architecture.
It does not validate accessibility with live devices or assistive technology.

use_cases.title: Choose the Right Presentation Pattern
use_cases.description: Compare an alert, sheet, popover, or action sheet for a specific product workflow.

use_cases.title: Review Destructive Confirmation Flows
use_cases.description: Check button labels, Cancel placement, and destructive styling before implementation.

use_cases.title: Audit Modal Friction
use_cases.description: Find unnecessary interruptions and replace them with inline feedback, undo, or progressive disclosure.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Component
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to show [situation] in an Apple app. Should I use an alert, sheet, popover, or action sheet?

prompt_templates.title: Rewrite Alert Copy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this alert title, message, and buttons against Apple HIG guidance: [paste copy].

prompt_templates.title: Design a Destructive Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design the confirmation flow for [destructive action]. Include presentation type, button labels, Cancel behavior, and alternatives.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Modal System
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit these modal and nonmodal presentations for an Apple app: [list flows]. Identify overuse, platform issues, and better patterns.

output_examples.input: A user taps Delete Account in settings.
output_examples.output: Recommended: alert. This is uncommon, destructive, and hard to undo. Use a clear title, brief consequence text, Delete Account as the destructive action, and Cancel as the safe escape.

output_examples.input: A user taps a toolbar filter button on iPad.
output_examples.output: Recommended: popover. The filter controls are temporary and related to the trigger. Keep it compact, point to the toolbar item, and save changes when it closes unless Cancel is explicit.

output_examples.input: A user cancels a draft message with unsaved text.
output_examples.output: Recommended: action sheet. Offer choices related to the user action, such as Save Draft, Delete Draft, and Cancel. Avoid a generic warning alert.

best_practices: Start by deciding whether the interruption is necessary for the user task.
Use specific verb labels for actions, especially destructive and confirmation buttons.
Adapt the presentation to platform conventions instead of forcing one pattern everywhere.
anti_patterns: Do not use alerts for routine information or common undoable actions.
Do not stack sheets, popovers, or other presentations over each other.
Do not use vague buttons like Yes, No, or OK for important decisions.

faq.question: What components does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers alerts, action sheets, popovers, sheets, and digit entry views from Apple HIG references.

faq.question: Can it write SwiftUI or UIKit code?
faq.answer: It can point to relevant APIs, but its main purpose is design guidance and interaction decisions.

faq.question: Does it apply outside Apple platforms?
faq.answer: The guidance is Apple-specific, but many interruption and confirmation principles can inform other platforms.

faq.question: When should I use an alert?
faq.answer: Use an alert for critical information, rare destructive confirmations, or situations that need immediate acknowledgment.

faq.question: When should I avoid a modal?
faq.answer: Avoid a modal when inline feedback, undo, a panel, or progressive disclosure can support the task without blocking users.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Manual review found documentation-only false positives and no executable malicious behavior.
